0201007.publishedDescription: Students must complete one of the following: PROGRAM PIN CERTIFICATION: Senior members enrolled in 4-H clubs deliver speeches and presentations, prepare demonstrations and educational displays and attend provincial 4-H conferences. To achieve a Program Pin Certification, senior 4-H members must either earn four program skill certificates with a score of 75 percent or higher or complete a Provincial 4-H Conference and 3 program skill certificates with a score of 75 percent or higher. SAFETY PIN CERTIFICATION: To receive a Safety Pin Certification, senior 4-H members must complete four of the eight 4-H Farm Safety Senior Skills (two from Part A and two from Part B) and score 75% or higher on each marked component. PROJECT PIN CERTIFICATION: To achieve a Project Pin Certification, senior 4-H members must enroll in a designated project following the completion of their junior proficiency certificate and receive four senior skill certificates in that project area. The four senior skill exams are both written and practical, with 75 percent required to pass each exam. - HST SUB Code: 0181

0201008.publishedDescription: The Honour Pin Certification is the highest 4-H award and is issued to senior members following completion of one of the following: two project pins, a project pin and program pin, or a project pin and a safety pin. - HST SUB Code: 0181

0202011.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Horse Council of BC. Examinations include a written or oral test, a riding test and a practical exam with a passing mark of 60% required for both the written and ridden tests. Assessment and evaluation must be administered by a current, certified coach 1 or higher. A minimum of 25 practical hours is required.

0202012.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Horse Council of BC. Examinations include a written or oral test, a riding test and a stable management/horse care test with a passing mark of 60% required for both the written and ridden tests. Assessment and evaluation must be administered by a current, certified coach 1 or higher. A minimum of 50 practical hours is required.

0202013.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Horse Council of BC. Examinations include a written or oral test, a riding test, a stable management/horse handling test and a lunging test with a passing mark of 60% required for both the written and ridden tests. Assessment and evaluation must be administered by a current, certified coach 2 or higher. A minimum of 50 practical hours is required.

0202019.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Horse Council of BC. Examinations include a written test and a practical exam with a passing mark of 70% required for both the written and ridden tests. Assessment and evaluation must be administered by two current, Equine Canada Western Coach 1 or higher. A minimum or 50 practical hours is required.

0202020.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Horse Council of BC. 0202021.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Horse Council of BC. 0202022.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Horse Council of BC. 0202025.publishedDescription: Once a student has successfully completed the course requirements, s/he will receive a Level 3 badge from the Equestrian Vaulting Association of BC and a Certificate of Completion from the Horse Council of British Columbia.

0901082.publishedDescription: New Media 10 is a program of studies designed to reflect the changing role of technology in today?s society and the increasing importance of digital media in communicating and exchanging ideas. This course is intended to allow students and educators the flexibility to develop a program of study centred on students? interests, needs, and abilities, while at the same time allowing for a range of local delivery methods. New Media 10 recognizes that digital literacy is an essential characteristic of the educated citizen. Coursework is aimed at providing students with a set of skills vital for success in an increasingly complex digital world by affording opportunities to demonstrate understanding and communicate ideas through a variety of digital and print media. New Media 10 explores tasks and texts designed to introduce students to the study of new media.

0901083.publishedDescription: New Media 11 is a program of studies designed to reflect the changing role of technology in today?s society and the increasing importance of digital media in communicating and exchanging ideas. This course is intended to allow students and educators the flexibility to develop an intensive program of study centred on students? interests, needs, and abilities, while at the same time allowing for a range of local delivery methods. New Media 11 recognizes that digital literacy is an essential characteristic of the educated citizen. Coursework is aimed at providing students with a set of skills vital for success in an increasingly complex digital world by affording numerous opportunities to demonstrate understanding and communicate increasingly sophisticated ideas through a wide variety of digital and print media. Compared with New Media 10, New Media 11 features tasks and texts of greater complexity and sophistication. As well, the Grade 11 course extends the depth and breadth of topics and activities offered in New Media 10.

0901084.publishedDescription: EFP New Media 10 is designed for students who are interested in exploring the increasing importance of digital media in communicating and exchanging ideas. This area of choice provides students with opportunities to think about the use of new media and its effects on individuals and on First Peoples communities and cultures. Students will work individually and collaboratively to develop skills needed in an increasingly complex digital world as they demonstrate understanding and communicate ideas through a variety of digital and print media.

0901085.publishedDescription: New Media 12 is a program of studies designed to reflect the changing role of technology in today?s society and the increasing importance of digital media in communicating and exchanging ideas. This course is intended to allow students and educators the flexibility to develop an intensive program of study centred on students? interests, needs, and abilities, while at the same time allowing for a range of local delivery methods. New Media 12 recognizes that digital literacy is an essential characteristic of the educated citizen. Coursework is aimed at providing students with a set of skills vital for success in an increasingly complex digital world by affording numerous opportunities to demonstrate understanding and communicate increasingly sophisticated ideas through a wide variety of digital and print media. Compared with New Media 11, New Media 12 features tasks and texts of greater complexity and sophistication. As well, the Grade 12 course extends the depth and breadth of topics and activities offered in New Media 11.

0901086.publishedDescription: Le cours de Médias et communication numérique 11 est conçu pour que l?élève explore, découvre, interprète et analyse l'évolution du rôle et de l?influence croissante des médias numériques et imprimés dans la société actuelle. Le cours reconnaît que la littératie numérique est essentielle au développement du citoyen numérique. Ce cours amènera l?élève à réfléchir de façon critique à la manière dont les médias changent et influencent les comportements en société, la manière de communiquer et la compréhension du monde. Le cours offre de nombreuses occasions à l?élève de démontrer sa compréhension et de communiquer ses idées à travers une variété de médias et communications numériques. De plus, il explorera les nouvelles possibilités d?expression créative qu?offrent les médias numériques. L?élève aura conscience des comportements en société conformes à la loi et à l?éthique afin de prendre des décisions réfléchies.

0901087.publishedDescription: Le cours de Médias et communication numérique 12 est conçu pour que l?élève interprète et analyse en profondeur le rôle et l?influence croissante des médias numériques et imprimés dans la société actuelle. Le cours reconnaît que la littératie numérique est essentielle au développement du citoyen numérique. Ce cours amènera l?élève à réfléchir de façon critique à la manière dont les médias changent et influencent les comportements en société, la manière de communiquer et la compréhension du monde. Le cours offre de nombreuses occasions à l?élève d?approfondir sa compréhension et de communiquer ses idées à travers une variété de médias et de communications numériques. De plus, il exploitera les nouvelles possibilités d?expression créative qu?offrent les médias numériques. L?élève aura conscience des comportements en société conformes à la loi et à l?éthique afin de prendre des décisions réfléchies.

1301454.publishedDescription: Youth Explore Trades Sampler (TST) is typically a 12 week, 300-hour program that consists of three four-credit courses. TST provides participants the opportunity to explore up to 5 trades. Training providers and School Districts have flexibility to offer the program full-time, or part-time over a longer period. The TST program is a SkilledTradesBC youth initiative that provides financial support to School Districts/Independent School Authorities to provide industry training opportunities for youth. TST specifically provides opportunities for youth to explore several trades and receive secondary school graduation credits.

1401038.publishedDescription: Engineering 11 introduces engineering design, product development, and manufacturing processes. Learning includes use of hand and power tools, programming languages for robotics, and approaches to innovative engineering projects.

1401039.publishedDescription: ENGINEERING 12 focuses on advanced aspects of engineering design, product development, and manufacturing processes. Learning includes static analysis of structures, geometric dimensioning and tolerancing, programming languages and applications, and robotics. Students also explore career opportunities and interpersonal and consultations skills related to client interactions.

1401433.publishedDescription: This course introduces simple robotics design and production, and related technical and programming elements. Learning includes interactions of subsystems, logic, mechanics, and components.

1401434.publishedDescription: This course focuses on advanced design, documentation, power and electronics, and applications of robotics technologies. Learning includes wiring, sensors, power management, and programming language. Students also explore emerging career opportunities in this field and interpersonal and consultation skills related to client interactions.

1601044.publishedDescription: The study of a second language and culture is intended to enable learners to communicate purposefully in the second language and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture and to develop openness to cultural diversity. Each Ministry Second Language 5 to 12 curriculum endorses what is commonly referred to as the communicative-experiential approach. In this approach the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, share ideas, acquire information, and get things done. Grammar instruction plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In following the communicative-experiential approach, prescribed learning outcomes in the curriculum are expressed in terms of tasks to be performed, not in terms of language items to be mastered. Assessment and evaluation of language acquisition focus on students' abilities to understand others and to express themselves comprehensibly and appropriately. Each language curriculum also includes an introductory Grade 11 course designed especially for students who have not previously studied that particular language in Grades 5 to 10. It is an intensive learning experience designed to provide students with an introduction to the language and culture being studied, and to provide a solid foundation for further study. Introductory Grade 11 incorporates material from the prescribed learning outcomes, suggested instructional strategies, suggested assessment strategies, and recommended learning resources identified for Grades 5 to 10. This reflects the fact that Introductory Grade 11 is designed to provide students with an equivalent preparation for Grade 11 and Grade 12 courses. A major consideration, therefore, is to relate the emerging language skills of students who are new to the study of the language to their actual ages, real-life experiences, and prior knowledge.

1601054.publishedDescription: Learning Korean as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in terms of the knowledge, language skills, and attitudes needed to understand and express messages effectively in Korean. Learning Korean will enhance the learning of first and additional languages. Thus, communicative competence attained in Korean can be a real source of personal satisfaction and enriches individual life learning experiences. British Columbia¿s proximity to the Far East is a prime reason for Western Canadian students to learn Korean. An understanding of Korean culture and society and the improvement of economic and commercial ties can be enhanced and enriched by communicative competence. Learning Korean can broaden the social and cultural perspectives of students and encourage respect for cultural diversity and its roots, including their own identity, and help personal growth. The Korean 5 to 12 curriculum endorses what is commonly referred to as the communicative-experiential approach. In this approach the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, share ideas, acquire information, and get things done. Grammar instruction plays a supportive role only¿to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension.

In this approach the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, share ideas, acquire information, and get things done. Grammar instruction plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In following the communicative-experiential approach, prescribed learning outcomes in the curriculum are expressed in terms of tasks to be performed, not in terms of language items to be mastered. Assessment and evaluation of language acquisition focus on students' abilities to understand others and to express themselves comprehensibly and appropriately. Each language curriculum also includes an introductory Grade 11 course designed especially for students who have not previously studied that particular language in Grades 5 to 10. It is an intensive learning experience designed to provide students with an introduction to the language and culture being studied, and to provide a solid foundation for further study. Introductory Grade 11 incorporates material from the prescribed learning outcomes, suggested instructional strategies, suggested assessment strategies, and recommended learning resources identified for Grades 5 to 10. This reflects the fact that Introductory Grade 11 is designed to provide students with an equivalent preparation for Grade 11 and Grade 12 courses. A major consideration, therefore, is to relate the emerging language skills of students who are new to the study of the language to their actual ages, real-life experiences, and prior knowledge.

1603039.publishedDescription: Learning Korean as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in terms of the knowledge, language skills, and attitudes needed to understand and express messages effectively in Korean. Learning Korean will enhance the learning of first and additional languages. Thus, communicative competence attained in Korean can be a real source of personal satisfaction and enriches individual life learning experiences. British Columbia¿s proximity to the Far East is a prime reason for Western Canadian students to learn Korean. An understanding of Korean culture and society and the improvement of economic and commercial ties can be enhanced and enriched by communicative competence. Learning Korean can broaden the social and cultural perspectives of students and encourage respect for cultural diversity and its roots, including their own identity, and help personal growth. The Korean 5 to 12 curriculum endorses what is commonly referred to as the communicative-experiential approach. In this approach the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, share ideas, acquire information, and get things done. Grammar instruction plays a supportive role only¿to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension.

1603078.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 5, students have the opportunity to hear, repeat, and "play" with language in Russian. Teachers give instructions in Russian. Students use drawings and other visual aids as well as non-verbal gestures to assist in communication. Students identify strategies to acquire information from English resources and learn to transfer these strategies to simple Russian language resources. They are able to present information in pictoral form and use individual Russian words and phrases. Students are exposed to Russian songs, rhymes, and stories. They respond to these works through drawing, acting, singing, dancing, and using simple language. Students participate in a variety of cultural experiences. The focus is on students' participation in identifying Russian regions in the world and their growing awareness of Russian culture in British Columbia. Students show their awareness in graphic and visual forms,with some commentary in English.

1603080.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 6, interaction with partners and small groups are more frequent. Pre-framed models help students express their ideas. Students are expected to take risks, attempting to use previously learned structures, and demonstrate their learning orally and through visual presentation. Students apply strategies such a predicting, connecting, and guessing from clues. They are able to select key ideas, along with selected details. They represent their understanding in a variety of ways that require minimal use of language. Students' comprehension of creative works and their visual and oral responses to it will rely heavily on visual and contextual support. Students talk and write about their own cultural experiences and take part in cultural activities. Their responses reveal the extent of their cultural understanding.

1603081.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 7 students are working with memorized language and can begin to connect ideas to form complete messages or short interactions. They use writing for reference purposes and develop their language skills, although written expression is not evaluated in Grade 7. While information tasks at this level continue to rely on visual representations, students are expected to use some basic, well-practised Russian vocabulary and language structures. Their growing communicative skills permit them to substitute language in the resources to suit their needs. The use of a Russian-English dictionary helps students to explore written material more independently and in greater depth. They experience and respond to an increasing range of creative works and use their growing range of strategies to make educated guesses at the meaning of new expressions. Students explore and experience aspects of Russian culture as well as aspects of their family and community traditions in order to to see the similarities that exist beneath surface differences.

1603082.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 8 students use Russian as a tool for communicating about everyday topics they enjoy talking about, such as themselves, their friends, and favourite activities. They practise and develop the language in new and interesting contexts and in different groupings - pairs, small and large groups, and individually. In Grade 8, writing skills are also assessed. Students acquire and use information to complete meaningful tasks of relevance to them. The purpose or task dictates the information needed. Students develop a strong interest in traditional and popular culture. They show an emerging ability to use Russian to express their thoughts, feelings, and reactions to creative works. At this level, students are encouraged to use Russian as they explore the similarities and differences in activities and interests that exist in Russian regions, thereby increasing their understanding of Russian culture.

1603085.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 9 the focus of learning is to convey and understand meaning for practical purposes in situations that are relevant to students at this level, such as preparing a meal or buying a gift. Some activities involve spontaneous communication, where students apply strategies for expressing and understanding meaning. Students need continued support to communicate with one another. Students show evidence of their language skills and strategies in the way they approach and work with resources, as well as the way they represent the information they have researched. Students experience and can respond to an increasing range of creative works, including music and other works of traditional and popular culture, in their oral, visual, and written responses. With students' deepening understanding of Russian culture, they are motivated to continue their language learning and add new perspectives to their views of the world.

1603087.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 9 the focus of learning is to convey and understand meaning for practical purposes in situations that are relevant to students at this level, such as preparing a meal or buying a gift. Some activities involve spontaneous communication, where students apply strategies for expressing and understanding meaning. Students need continued support to communicate with one another. Students show evidence of their language skills and strategies in the way they approach and work with resources, as well as the way they represent the information they have researched. Students experience and can respond to an increasing range of creative works, including music and other works of traditional and popular culture, in their oral, visual, and written responses. With students' deepening understanding of Russian culture, they are motivated to continue their language learning and add new perspectives to their views of the world.

1603088.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. For some students, this may be their first opportunity to study Russian. To develop effective Russian language skills, students focus on communicating an increasing range of information, beginning with their interests, experiences, and information needs. As students progress and gain confidence in their Russian language skills, they apply a growing range of language-learning strategies and begin to take risks with the language. Students use language-learning strategies to extract key information from authentic Russian documents and other materials to complete relevant tasks which are expressed in age-appropriate formats. At this age, students are able to experience a range of creative works, including examples from outside class. Students' abilities to share and elaborate on their views and responses increase. Students participate in a variety of cultural experiences, with a focus on Russian culture. Students have frequent opportunities to interact in the Russian language in order to practise using appropriate communication conventions.

1603089.publishedDescription: The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 11 students are able to interact with greater confidence in familiar situations and apply their growing range of strategies more consistently. Self- and peer assessment are important ways of supporting students as they acquire and practise useful vocabulary, structures, and interactions. Students are familiar with a wide range of classroom, library, and other community resources they can use to locate information. The forms and skills students use to apply and convey information for particular purposes and audiences are increasingly important. Students' oral and written skills should enable them to respond to creative works from a broader range of genres, time periods, and Russian regions. Students' developing language skills allow them to appreciate these creative works and their cultural contexts more fully and through oral interactions and short, written texts. As students' understanding of the Russian world deepens, students will recognize how Russian and English languages and cultures influence one another. Through the study of Russian culture and reflection on other cultures, students enhance their sensitivity toward other cultures as a whole. At this level, the complexity of students' ideas will far exceed their ability to express them in Russian. Opportunities to communicate using symbols, graphics, or diagrams in addition to Russian elicit higher-level thinking.

1603090.publishedDescription: Learning Russian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire a new set of competencies in a language that will enable them to benefit from expanded national and international career opportunities in many fields such as information technology, manufacturing, commerce, and tourism. The Russian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 12 students are expected to engage in increasingly complex and spontaneous oral interactions in which they demonstrate their facility in Russian and the strategies they have developed to sustain and extend communication in familiar as well as in unexpected situations. Students are able to locate information with confidence, skim for relevant facts, and use Russian resources in their community and elsewhere, e.g., via the internet or correspondence. As students develop increasing facility with oral and written language, they experience and can respond to both contemporary and traditional works in varied ways, with increasing detail and independence. Students examine Russian culture in light of all their cultural experiences. At this level, students should be able to show considerable cultural awareness and sensitivity when communicating in Russian.

1603091.publishedDescription: Learning Croatian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire or extend their competencies in a Slavic language from a region that is becoming a very popular tourist destination in Europe. Other benefits include the opportunity for some students to study Croatian as a heritage language and to enable further university studies in Croatian or Slavic cultural studies. The Croatian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 5, students have the opportunity to hear, repeat, and "play" with language in Croatian. Teachers give instructions in Croatian. Students use drawings and other visual aids as well as non-verbal gestures to assist in communication. Students identify strategies to acquire information from English resources and learn to transfer these strategies to simple Russian language resources. They are able to present information in pictorial form and use individual Croatian words and phrases. Students are exposed to Croatian songs, rhymes, and stories. They respond to these works through drawing, acting, singing, dancing, and using simple language. Students participate in a variety of cultural experiences. The focus is on students' growing awareness of Croatian culture in British Columbia. Students show their awareness in graphic and visual forms, with some commentary in English.

Opportunities to communicate using symbols, graphics, or diagrams in addition to Croatian elicit higher-level thinking.

1603098.publishedDescription: Learning Croatian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire or extend their competencies in a Slavic language from a region that is becoming a very popular tourist destination in Europe. Other benefits include the opportunity for some students to study Croatian as a heritage language and to enable further university studies in Croatian or Slavic cultural studies. The Croatian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 12 students are expected to engage in increasingly complex and spontaneous oral interactions in which they demonstrate their facility in Croatian and the strategies they have developed to sustain and extend communication in familiar as well as in unexpected situations. Students are able to locate information with confidence, skim for relevant facts, and use Croatian resources in their community and elsewhere, e.g., via the internet or correspondence. As students develop increasing facility with oral and written language, they experience and can respond to both contemporary and traditional works in varied ways, with increasing detail and independence. Students examine Croatian culture in light of all their cultural experiences. At this level, students should be able to show considerable cultural awareness and sensitivity when communicating in Croatian.

1603099.publishedDescription: Learning Croatian as a second language offers students the opportunity to acquire or extend their competencies in a Slavic language from a region that is becoming a very popular tourist destination in Europe. Other benefits include the opportunity for some students to study Croatian as a heritage language and to enable further university studies in Croatian or Slavic cultural studies. The Croatian 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. For some students, this may be their first opportunity to study Croatian. To develop effective Croatian language skills, students focus on communicating an increasing range of information, beginning with their interests, experiences, and information needs. As students progress and gain confidence in their Russian language skills, they apply a growing range of language-learning strategies and begin to take risks with the language. Students use language-learning strategies to extract key information from authentic Croatian documents and other materials to complete relevant tasks which are expressed in age-appropriate formats. At this age, students are able to experience a range of creative works, including examples from outside class. Students' abilities to share and elaborate on their views and responses increase. Students participate in a variety of cultural experiences, with a focus on Croatian culture Students have frequent opportunities to interact in Croatian in order to practise using appropriate communication conventions.

1603106.publishedDescription: The study of a second language and culture is intended to enable learners to communicate purposefully in the second language and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture and to develop openness to cultural diversity. Each Ministry Second Language 5 to 12 curriculum endorses what is commonly referred to as the communicative-experiential approach. In this approach the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, share ideas, acquire information, and get things done. Grammar instruction plays a supportive role only¿to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In following the communicative-experiential approach, prescribed learning outcomes in the curriculum are expressed in terms of tasks to be performed, not in terms of language items to be mastered. Assessment and evaluation of language acquisition focus on students' abilities to understand others and to express themselves comprehensibly and appropriately. Each language curriculum also includes an introductory Grade 11 course designed especially for students who have not previously studied that particular language in Grades 5 to 10. It is an intensive learning experience designed to provide students with an introduction to the language and culture being studied, and to provide a solid foundation for further study. Introductory Grade 11 incorporates material from the prescribed learning outcomes, suggested instructional strategies, suggested assessment strategies, and recommended learning resources identified for Grades 5 to 10. This reflects the fact that Introductory Grade 11 is designed to provide students with an equivalent preparation for Grade 11 and Grade 12 courses. A major consideration, therefore, is to relate the emerging language skills of students who are new to the study of the language to their actual ages, real-life experiences, and prior knowledge.

1605053.publishedDescription: A reciprocal exchange program is available for a limited number of Grade 10/11 French Immersion and francophone Program students. Quebec students live with BC families for three months and attend BC schools. Later in the school year, BC students live with Quebec families for three months and attend Quebec schools. Students apply through participating schools. In order to earn credit, students will present a letter indicating successful completion of the program.

1605055.publishedDescription: THE CURRICULUM FOR FRANÇAIS LANGUE PREMIÈRE K-12 IS DESIGNED AS A FIRST-LANGUAGE PROGRAM FOR FRANCOPHONE STUDENTS QUALIFYING UNDER SECTION 23 OF THE CANADIAN CHARTER OF RIGHTS AND FREEDOMS. IT AIMS TO DEVELOP AND MAINTAIN A SENSE OF CULTURAL IDENTITY IN FRANCOPHONE STUDENTS, AND TO PROVIDE THESE STUDENTS WITH OPPORTUNITIES FOR PERSONAL AND INTELLECTUAL GROWTH TO PREPARE THEM TO PARTICIPATE EFFECTIVELY IN ALL ASPECTS OF SOCIETY. THE LEARNING OUTCOMES ARE GROUPED INTO THREE MAIN ORGANIZERS: ORAL COMMUNICATION, READING AND VIEWING, AND WRITING AND REPRESENTING.

THE LEARNING OUTCOMES ARE GROUPED INTO THREE MAIN ORGANIZERS: ORAL COMMUNICATION, READING AND VIEWING, AND WRITING AND REPRESENTING.

1607011.publishedDescription: Through the Français langue seconde - immersion 10 curriculum, students continue to build their personal, cultural and linguistic identity as speakers of French as a second or additional language. The curriculum views the French language as a tool of reflection and communication that will eventually allow students to contribute to society.

1607012.publishedDescription: Le cours de Français langue immersion 12 est requis pour l?obtention du diplôme bilingue. L?objectif de ce cours est d?intégrer et d?approfondir les compétences langagières et les connaissances linguistiques et culturelles acquises lors des expériences d?apprentissage antérieures. Ce cours permet à l?élève d?avoir un aperçu des divers facteurs qui façonnent son identité bilingue. Le cours offre de nombreuses occasions à l?élève d?approfondir la connaissance de soi et des autres dans un monde en évolution. De plus, l?élève explorera des textes de plusieurs genres et modes, provenant de sources diverses et reflétant des perspectives de la francophonie et des peuples autochtones. Ce cours amènera l?élève à utiliser sa pensée critique et créative pour analyser diverses situations de communication. Finalement, l?élève affinera sa communication dans divers contextes afin d?atteindre ses objectifs personnels et professionnels.

1608005.publishedDescription: THE FRENCH IMMERSION PROGRAM IS AN INTENSIVE SECOND-LANGUAGE PROGRAM DESIGNED TO PRODUCE FUNCTIONALLY BILINGUAL STUDENTS BY USING FRENCH AS THE LANGUAGE OF INSTRUCTION. THE LEARNING OUTCOMES OF THE LANGUAGE ARTS IRPS ARE GROUPED INTO 3 MAIN ORGANIZERS: COMMUNICATIONS (ALLOWS STUDENTS TO INTERACT, TO COMPREHEND, AND TO RESPOND TO LITERARY AND INFORMATIONAL COMMUNICATIONS, AND TO COMMUNICATE IDEAS AND INFORMATION), CULTURE (ALLOWS STUDENTS TO VALUE THEIR OWN AND OTHER CULTURES, INCLUDING CULTURES OF THE FRENCH-SPEAKING WORLD), AND SELF AND SOCIETY (ALLOWS STUDENTS TO DEVELOP CONFIDENCE, TO THINK CREATIVELY AND CRITICALLY, AND TO USE LANGUAGE TO WORK WITH OTHERS).

1608006.publishedDescription: THE FRENCH IMMERSION PROGRAM IS AN INTENSIVE SECOND-LANGUAGE PROGRAM DESIGNED TO PRODUCE FUNCTIONALLY BILINGUAL STUDENTS BY USING FRENCH AS THE LANGUAGE OF INSTRUCTION. 1608010.publishedDescription: Core French 10 focuses on developing communication competencies in the French language, exploring Francophone communities and cultures within Canada and around the world, exploring identity, and engaging in the range of opportunities and experiences that developing proficiency in French provides.

1609001.publishedDescription: Core French 11 focuses on developing communication competencies in the French language, exploring Francophone communities and cultures within Canada and around the world, exploring identity, and engaging in the range of opportunities and experiences that developing proficiency in French provides.

1609004.publishedDescription: Italian Language Competency Assessment - listening, speaking, reading and writing.

1609006.publishedDescription: The study of a second language and culture is intended to enable learners to communicate purposefully in the second language and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture and to develop openness to cultural diversity. Each Ministry Second Language 5 to 12 curriculum endorses what is commonly referred to as the communicative-experiential approach. In this approach the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, share ideas, acquire information, and get things done. Grammar instruction plays a supportive role only¿to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In following the communicative-experiential approach, prescribed learning outcomes in the curriculum are expressed in terms of tasks to be performed, not in terms of language items to be mastered. Assessment and evaluation of language acquisition focus on students' abilities to understand others and to express themselves comprehensibly and appropriately. Each language curriculum also includes an introductory Grade 11 course designed especially for students who have not previously studied that particular language in Grades 5 to 10. It is an intensive learning experience designed to provide students with an introduction to the language and culture being studied, and to provide a solid foundation for further study. Introductory Grade 11 incorporates material from the prescribed learning outcomes, suggested instructional strategies, suggested assessment strategies, and recommended learning resources identified for Grades 5 to 10. This reflects the fact that Introductory Grade 11 is designed to provide students with an equivalent preparation for Grade 11 and Grade 12 courses. A major consideration, therefore, is to relate the emerging language skills of students who are new to the study of the language to their actual ages, real-life experiences, and prior knowledge.

1609059.publishedDescription: Explore Program - This program covering room, board and tuition enables Anglophone students 16 years and older to enroll in five-week immersion courses in French. For detailed information, please visit www.englishfrench.ca. To earn credit, students will present a letter from the Explore institution attended, indicating successful completion.

1609061.publishedDescription: Intensive French is an intensive French language acquisition program involving a period of intensive exposure to French(80% of one half of the grade 6 year and 20% for the remaining half). Enrichment of the regular Core French curriculum is provided through expanding the content of the curriculum, increasing the depth of exploration of topics, and adopting a whole language approach to the teaching of French. Intensive French integrates some information form other subject areas, such as Science (environmental issues), Social Studies (Canadian and world geography, Canadian and world history) and Health (rules for good nutrition).

1609062.publishedDescription: Intensive French 7 is a follow-up program in which students experience an enriched French curriculum, enabling them to be in a position to successfully complete Grade 12 Core French in a timely manner. Intensive French provides students with opportunities for more electives during the graduation years.

1609169.publishedDescription: A summary of groups of instructional programs that describe the structure and use of language common or indigenous to people of the same community or nation, the same geographical area, or cultural traditions; including such features as sounds, literature, syntax, phonology, morphology, semantics, sentences, prose and verse, as well as development of skills and attitudes used in communicating and evaluating thoughts and feelings through oral and written language. (Spanish Language Competency Assessment - listening, speaking, reading and writing.)

1609200.publishedDescription: The study of a second language and culture is intended to enable learners to communicate purposefully in the second language and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture and to develop openness to cultural diversity. Each Ministry Second Language 5 to 12 curriculum endorses what is commonly referred to as the communicative-experiential approach. In this approach the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, share ideas, acquire information, and get things done. Grammar instruction plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In following the communicative-experiential approach, prescribed learning outcomes in the curriculum are expressed in terms of tasks to be performed, not in terms of language items to be mastered. Assessment and evaluation of language acquisition focus on students' abilities to understand others and to express themselves comprehensibly and appropriately. Each language curriculum also includes an introductory Grade 11 course designed especially for students who have not previously studied that particular language in Grades 5 to 10. It is an intensive learning experience designed to provide students with an introduction to the language and culture being studied, and to provide a solid foundation for further study. Introductory Grade 11 incorporates material from the prescribed learning outcomes, suggested instructional strategies, suggested assessment strategies, and recommended learning resources identified for Grades 5 to 10. This reflects the fact that Introductory Grade 11 is designed to provide students with an equivalent preparation for Grade 11 and Grade 12 courses. A major consideration, therefore, is to relate the emerging language skills of students who are new to the study of the language to their actual ages, real-life experiences, and prior knowledge.

1610147.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing pportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. Grade five students have the opportunity to hear, repeat, and "play" in Nte?kepmxcin. Teachers give instructions in the target language and students use drawings and other visual aids as well as non-verbal gestures to assist in communication. Students identify strategies to acquire information from Nte?kepmxcin resources and are able to present information in pictoral form and use individual Nte?kepmxcin words and phrases. Students are exposed to a variety of Nte?kepmxcin creative works and respond to them though drawing,singing, dancing, acting, and using simple language. Students also participate in a variety of cultural experiences based on their own heritage. They show their awareness in graphic and visual forms, with some commentary in English and in Nte?kepmxcin.

1610148.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In grade 6, interaction with partners and small groups are more frequent. Pre-framed models help students express their ideas. Students are expected to take risks, attempting to use previously learned structures, and demonstrate their learning orally and through visual presentation. Students apply strategies such a predicting, connecting, and guessing from clues. They are able to select key ideas, along with selected details. They represent their understanding in a variety of ways that require minimal use of language. Students' comprehension of creative works and their visual and oral responses to it will rely heavily on visual and contextual support. Students talk and write about their own cultural experiences and take part in cultural activities. Their responses reveal the extent of their cultural understanding.

1610149.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 7 students are working with memorized language and can begin to connect ideas to form complete messages or short interactions. They use writing for reference purposes and develop their language skills, although written expression is not evaluated in Grade 7. While information tasks at this level continue to rely on visual representations, students are expected to use some basic, well-practised Nte?kepmxcin vocabulary and language structures. Their growing communicative skills permit them to substitute language in the resources to suit their needs. They experience and respond to an increasing range of creative works. Students explore and experience the Nte?kepmxcin presence, both historical and current in British Columbia and Canada.

1610150.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 8 students use Nte?kepmxcin as a tool for communicating about everyday topics they enjoy talking about, such as themselves, their friends, and favourite activities. They practise and develop the language in new and interesting contexts and in different groupings - pairs, small and large groups, and individually. In Grade 8, writing skills are also assessed. Students acquire and use information to complete meaningful tasks. The purpose or task dictates the information needed. Students develop a strong interest in traditional and popular culture. They show and emerging ability to use Nte?kepmxcin to express their thoughts, feelings, and reactions to creative works. At this level, students are encouraged to use Nte?kepmxcin in activities that support their cultural awareness. Their understanding of Nte?kepmxcin culture continues to deepen.

1610151.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 9 the focus of learning is to convey and understand meaning for practical purposes in situations that are meaningful to students at this level, such as ordering a meal or buying a gift. Some activities involve spontaneous communication, where students apply strategies for expressing and understanding meaning. Students need continued support to communicate with one another. Students show evidence of their language skills and strategies in the way they approach and work with resources, as well as the way the represent the information they have researched. Students experience and can respond to an increasing range of creative works, including music and other works of traditional and popular culture, in their oral, visual, and written responses. Students build on their knowledge of Nte?mxcin cultural practices.

1610152.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 10 students are increasingly able to take risks while engaging in spontaneous interactions using memorized vocabulary and structures. In addition, students are developing descriptive abilities that include linking and sequencing of narrative. Students are able to work with an increasing variety of resources to locate information to complete a task. Students continue to experience a range of creative works and as their facility with language increases, they are able to provide more detailed oral, visual, and written responses. Students examine the role of cultural practices and traditional language patterns that help to shape their identity. As students talk and write about their developing knowledge, they show increasing awareness about cultural comparisons.

1610153.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. For some students, this may be their first opportunity to study Nte?kepmxcin. Students hear, repeat, and "play" in Nte?kepmxcin. Students use drawings and other visual aids as well as non-verbal gestures to assist in communication. Teachers give instructions in Nte?kepmxcin. Students identify strategies used to acquire information from English resources and learn to transfer these strategies to simple Nte?kepmxcin resources. As the course progresses, they are able to present information in simple sentence and paragraph formats. Students are exposed to various types of Nte?kepmxcin creative works and respond to them though singing, drawing, acting, dancing and simple language, both oral and written. Students actively compare a variety of cultural experiences based on their own heritage, using a variety of formats.

1610154.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 11 students are able to interact with greater confidence in familiar situations and apply their growing range of strategies more consistently. Self and peer assessment are important ways of supporting students as they acquire and practise useful vocabulary, structures, and interactions. Students are familiar with a wide range of classroom, library, and other community resources they can use to can use to locate information. The forms and skills students use to apply and convey information for particular purposes and audiences are increasingly important. Students' oral and written skills enable them to respond to creative works in increasingly sophisticated ways, through oral interactions, journal entries, résumés, and short written texts. At this level, the complexity of students' ideas may far exceed their ability to express them in Nte?kepmxcin. As their knowledge of Nte?kepmxcin culture deepens, so does their ability to make observations about other cultures.

1610155.publishedDescription: The study of Nte?kepmxcin and culture enables learners to communicate in Nte?kepmxcin by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, and to develop an openness to cultural diversity. The Nte?kepmxcin 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 12 students are expected to engage in increasingly complex and spontaneous oral interactions in which they demonstrate their facility in Nte?kepmxcin and the strategies they have developed to sustain and extend communication. Students are able to locate information with confidence, skim for relevant facts, and use Nte?kepmxcin resources in their community and elsewhere, e.g., via the internet or correspondence. As students develop increasing facility with oral and written language, they experience and can respond to both contemporary and traditional works in a variety of genres. The outcomes for cultural understanding emphasize awareness and sensitivity. Students reveal their cultural understanding though daily activities and interactions, as well as in completed assignments.

1610165.publishedDescription: The study of Nuu-chah-nulth language and culture enables learners to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. The focus is on the Nuu-chah-nulth regions on Vancouver Island to encourage a growing awareness of Nuu-chah-nulth culture. Students show their awareness in graphic and visual forms,with some commentary in English.

The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done.
