In Grade 7 students are working with memorized language and can begin to connect ideas to form complete messages or short interactions. They use writing for reference purposes and develop their language skills, although written expression is not evaluated in Grade 7. While information tasks at this level continue to rely on visual representations, students are expected to use some basic, well-practised Nuu-chah-nulth vocabulary and language structures. Their growing communicative skills permit them to substitute language in the resources to suit their needs. The use of a Nuu-chah-nulth-English dictionary helps students to explore written material more independently and in greater depth. They experience and respond to an increasing range of creative works and use their growing range of strategies to make educated guesses at the meaning of new expressions. Students explore and experience aspects of Nuu-chah-nulth culture as well as aspects of their family and community traditions in order to to see the similarities that exist beneath surface differences.

1610168.publishedDescription: The study of Nuu-chah-nulth language and culture enables learners to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 8 students use Nuu-chah-nulth as a tool for communicating about everyday topics they enjoy talking about, such as themselves, their friends, and favourite activities. They practise and develop the language in new and interesting contexts and in different groupings - pairs, small and large groups, and individually. In Grade 8, writing skills are also assessed. Students acquire and use information to complete meaningful tasks of relevance to them. The purpose or task dictates the information needed. Students develop a strong interest in traditional and popular culture. They show an emerging ability to use Nuu-chah-nulth to express their thoughts, feelings, and reactions to creative works. At this level, students are encouraged to use Nuu-chah-nulth as they explore the similarities and differences in activities and interests that exist in Nuu-chah-nulth regions, thereby increasing their understanding of Nuu-chah-nulth culture.

1610169.publishedDescription: The study of Nuu-chah-nulth language and culture enables learners to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 9 the focus of learning is to convey and understand meaning for practical purposes in situations that are relevant to students at this level, such as preparing a meal or buying a gift. Some activities involve spontaneous communication, where students apply strategies for expressing and understanding meaning. Students need continued support to communicate with one another. Students show evidence of their language skills and strategies in the way they approach and work with resources, as well as the way they represent the information they have researched. Students experience and can respond to an increasing range of creative works, including music and other works of traditional and popular culture, in their oral, visual, and written responses. With students' deepening understanding of Nuu-chah-nulth culture, they are motivated to continue their language learning and add new perspectives to their views of the world.

1610170.publishedDescription: The study of Nuu-chah-nulth language and culture enables learners to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 10 students are increasingly able to take risks while engaging in spontaneous interactions using memorized vocabulary and structures. In addition, students are developing descriptive abilities that include linking and sequencing of narrative. Students are able to use many strategies to identify key information in authentic documents, recordings, and fluent speech. The tasks students perform frequently integrate all aspects of their language learning. Students are able to appreciate a range of creative works and may bring in examples from outside the class. They are able to provide more detailed oral, visual, and written responses. Students examine the role of cultural practices and traditional language patterns that help to shape their identity. As students talk and write about their developing knowledge, they show increasing awareness of Nuu-chah-nulth cultural practices and expressions. They reflect on and make comparisons among Nuu-chah-nulth culture, Canadian cultures, their own cultures, and the cultures of their friends.

1610171.publishedDescription: The study of Nuu-chah-nulth language and culture enables learners to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 12 students are expected to engage in increasingly complex and spontaneous oral interactions in which they demonstrate their facility in Nuu-chah-nulth and the strategies they have developed to sustain and extend communication in common as well as in unexpected situations. Students are able to locate information with confidence, skim for relevant facts, and use Nuu-chah-nulth resources in their community and elsewhere, e.g., via the internet or correspondence. As students develop increasing facility with oral and written language, they experience and can respond to both contemporary and traditional works in varied ways, with increasing detail and independence. Students examine traditional and contemporary Nuu-chah-nulth culture in light of all their cultural experiences. At this level, students should be able to show considerable cultural awareness and sensitivity when communicating in Nuu-chah-nulth.

1610172.publishedDescription: The study of Nuu-chah-nulth language and culture enables learners to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to communicate in Nuu-chah-nulth, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. For some students, this may be their first opportunity to study Nuu-chah-nulth. To develop effective Nuu-chah-nulth language skills, students focus on communicating an increasing range of information, beginning with their interests, experiences, and information needs. As students progress and gain confidence in their Nuu-chah-nulth language skills, they apply a growing range of language-learning strategies and begin to take risks with the language. Students use language-learning strategies to extract key information from authentic Nuu-chah-nulth documents and other materials to complete relevant tasks which are expressed in age-appropriate formats. At this age, students experience a range of creative works, including examples from outside class. Students' abilities to share and elaborate on their views and responses increase. Students participate in a variety of cultural experiences, with a focus on Nuu-chah-nulth culture Students have frequent opportunities to interact in the Nuu-chah-nulth languagae in order to practise using appropriate communication conventions.

1610173.publishedDescription: The Nuu-chah-nulth 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 11 students are able to interact with greater confidence in familiar situations and apply their growing range of strategies more consistently. Self- and peer assessment are important ways of supporting students as they acquire and practise useful vocabulary, structures, and interactions. Students are familiar with a wide range of classroom, library, and other community resources they can use to can use to locate information. The forms and skills students use to apply and convey information for particular purposes and audiences are increasingly important. Students' oral and written skills should enable them to respond to creative works from a broader range of genres, time periods, and Nuu-chah-nulth regions. Students' developing language skills allow them to appreciate these creative works and their cultural contexts more fully and through oral interactions and short, written texts. As students' understanding of Nuu-chah-nulth culture deepens, students will recognize how Nuu-chah-nulth and English languages and cultures influence one another. Through the study of Nuu-chah-nulth culture and reflection on other cultures, students enhance their sensitivity toward other cultures as a whole. At this level, the complexity of students' ideas will far exceed their ability to express them in Nuu-chah-nulth. Opportunities to communicate using symbols, graphics, or diagrams in addition to Nuu-chah-nulth elicits higher-level thinking.

1610174.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and culture enables learners to communicate in Kwak'wala by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 5, students have the opportunity to hear, repeat, and play in Kwak'wala. Teachers give instructions in the target language. Students use drawings and other visual aids as well as non-verbal gestures to assist in communication. The building blocks for organizing ideas into connected speech or text are introduced in Grade 5, as students respond with basic words, phrases and sentences, recognize time expressions, and begin to organize ideas by producing lists. Students already use a variety of strategies for accessing information in their first language and learn to transfer these strategies to simple Kwak'wala language resources. They are able to present information in pictoral form and use individual Kwak'wala words and phrases. Students are exposed to Kwak'wala songs, rhymes, and stories. They respond to these works through drawing, acting, singing, dancing, and using simple language. Students participate in a variety of cultural experiences. The focus is on students' participation in cultural activities and their increasing awareness of Kwakwaka'wakw culture.. Students show their awareness in graphic and visual forms,with some commentary in English.

1610175.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 6 students' enjoyment of the language is a continuing priority. Learning activities focus on students' participation in speaking, listening, and viewing. Interactions with partners and small groups are more frequent. Pre-framed models continue to help students express their ideas. At this level students move on from the basic phrases internalized in Grade 6 to independently create phrases and simple sentences using new vocabulary. They begin to take risks to individualize their work. In Grade 6 students' ability to use Kwak'wala is minimal, but their ability to access information in their first language and display it visually is already quite developed. As students apply strategies such s predicting, connecting, and guessing, they experience success and develop confidence in working with Kwak'wala materials. As students begin to make generalizations about what they see and hear, and to connect their experiences of Kwakwaka'wakw creative works to those of other cultures. As students talk and write about their own cultural experiences and engage in classroom cultural activities, they reveal the extent of their openness and interest through their participation and questions they ask.

1610176.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 7 students work with some memorized language and begin to connect ideas to form short interactions. They use writing for reference purposes and to develop their language skills, although accuracy of written expression is not assessed in grade 7. At this level, students begin to build on expressions and language they have acquired to produce connected messages. While many of the information tasks at this level continue to rely on visual representations, students are expected to use some basic well-practiced Kwak'wala vocabulary and language structures. Growing communicative skills permit them to begin to transfer and substitute language in the resources to suit their own purposes. Students in Grade 7 can recognize familiar language and can sometimes use their growing range of strategies to make educated guesses of the meaning of new expressions. Students benefit by having the opportunity to choose they way they respond to painting, song, dance,or video. Grade 7 students continue to explore and experience aspects of the Kwakwaka'wakew world as well as aspects of their family, school, and community traditions in order to see similarities that exist beneath surface differences.

1610177.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 8 students use Kwak'wala as a tool for communicating about every day topics they enjoy, such as themselves, their friends, and favourite activities. Students need a safe environment for language risk-taking and opportunities to practice and develop their language in new contexts and different groupings. At this level, students begin to use writing to express messages about everyday topics of interest, and use time expressions and conjunctions to link test or speech together. Grade 8 students are interested in finding information about things when there is a meaningful reason to do so. The purpose or task dictates what information is needed and students demonstrate their skills and strategies by how they use and present the information to complete the task. At this age, students benefit from opportunities to experience works by a variety of Kwakwaka'wakw artists and storytellers. They use their growing language skills to select and discuss their favourite characters or works of art. Grade 8 students explore the similarities and differences between traditional and contemporary activities and interests in the Kwakwaka'wakw world to increase their understanding of Kwakwaka'wakw culture.

1610178.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 9 students continue to focus on the communication of meaning, with increasing focus on student interaction. The focus of learning is is to convey and understand meaning of practical purposes in situations that are relevant to Grade 9 students, such as preparing a meal or buying a gift. At this level, grade 9 students continue to expand their range of techniques for producing connected messages to convey meaning. When involved in spontaneous speaking activities, the focus is on participation, enthusiasm, and willingness to take risk with language. Students use their language skills and learning strategies to acquire age-relevant information for practical purposes. Students are able to experience and respond to an increasing range of creative works. They reveal their development in the choices they make and by efforts to use Kwak'wala in informal situations, as well as in their oral, visual, and written work. With students' deepening understanding of Kwakwaka'wakw culture, they are motivated to continue their language learning and add new perspectives to thier views of the world.

1610179.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. Students in Grade 10 are increasingly able to engage in spontaneous interactions using vocabulary and structures they have internalized. They display a growing ability to take risks with language. Communicating meaning is still the central focus, with emphasis on practical and everyday use of meaning. Students' descriptive abilities at this level include linking and sequencing of narratives to better communicate meaning, using learned structures and vocabulary in new ways in spontaneous presentations. At this level students are able to use many strategies to identify key information in a variety of source materials. The tasks they perform frequently integrate all aspects of language learning. As students develop increasing facility with oral language, their methods of responding to creative works include artwork, visual displays, and oral or electronic presentations. Grade 10 learning activities support a growing awareness of Kwakwaka'wakww cultural practices and Kwak'wala idiomatic expressions.

1610180.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners to communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Introductory Kwak'wala 11, students begin to develop effective Kwak'wala language skills by focusing on an exchanging an increasing range of information, beginning with their interests and needs. As students progress in the Kwak'wala skills they apply a growing range of language learning strategies. Growing language skills enable students to gain facility in using a range of structures and sequencing techniques to produce connected speech and text. Introductory Kwak'wala 11 students are encouraged to use as many language-learning strategies as they can to extract key information from Kwak'wal resources to complete tasks. At this age, students can experience a much wider range of creative works, particularly stories and songs, that they are able to understand independently. Students are encouraged to respond creatively from a variety of options. Introductory Grade 11 students are encouraged to participate in a range of Kwakwaka'wakw cultural experiences. As their language abilities grow, students have frequent opportunities to interact in Kwak'wala and practice using appropriate communication conventions.

1610181.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. Students in Grade 11 are able to act with greater confidence in familiar situations and apply their growing range of communications strategies more consistently. At this level, students are able to speak and write with growing confidence and experiment with an increasing range of structures and sequencing techniques for practical purposes. By grade 11, students are familiar with a wide range of classroom, library, and other resources they can use to locate information. Many students are able to retrieve specific information and apply their growing language abilities to adapting information for a purpose. At this age, students are increasingly receptive to experiencing and interpreting creative works from a broader range of formats, time periods, and Kwakwaka'wakw regions. Through oral interactions and short written texts, students demonstrate and increasing level of sophistication in their responses.

1610182.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 12, students are expected to engage in increasingly complex and spontaneous oral interactions in which they demonstrate their facility with Kwak'wala and the strategies they have developed to sustain and extend communication. Students are expected to produce increasingly complex connected speech and text by bringing together the writing and speaking skills they have learned and apply them to real-life situations. Grade 12 students are be able to locate information with confidence, skim for relevant facts, and use dictionaries and other resources appropriately. As students develop increasing facility with oral and written language, they are able to experience and interpret traditional and contemporary creative works in varied ways, with increasing detail and independence. In grade 12, students reveal their cultural understanding through daily activities and interactions by examining traditional and contemporary Kwakwaka'wakw culture in light of all their cultural experiences.

1610184.publishedDescription: The new SENCOTEN 5 to 12 Integrated Resource Package (2012), which also includes a Grade 11 Introductory course, has been developed using the communicative-experiential approach to language learning, a methodology which promotes relevant and personal use of the language to communicate information. 1610203.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and culture enables learners to communicate in Kwak'wala by providing opportunities for students to gain insights into their own culture, to revitalize the language, and to pass it on to future generations. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 5, students have the opportunity to hear, repeat, and play in Kwak'wala. Teachers give instructions in the target language. Students use drawings and other visual aids as well as non-verbal gestures to assist in communication. The building blocks for organizing ideas into connected speech or text are introduced in Grade 5, as students respond with basic words, phrases and sentences, recognize time expressions, and begin to organize ideas by producing lists. Students already use a variety of strategies for accessing information in their first language and learn to transfer these strategies to simple Kwak'wala language resources. They are able to present information in pictoral form and use individual Kwak'wala words and phrases. Students are exposed to Kwak'wala songs, rhymes, and stories. They respond to these works through drawing, acting, singing, dancing, and using simple language. Students participate in a variety of cultural experiences. The focus is on students' participation in cultural activities and their increasing awareness of Kwakwaka'wakw culture.. Students show their awareness in graphic and visual forms,with some commentary in English.

1610204.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 6 students' enjoyment of the language is a continuing priority. Learning activities focus on students' participation in speaking, listening, and viewing. Interactions with partners and small groups are more frequent. Pre-framed models continue to help students express their ideas. At this level students move on from the basic phrases internalized in Grade 6 to independently create phrases and simple sentences using new vocabulary. They begin to take risks to individualize their work. In Grade 6 students' ability to use Kwak'wala is minimal, but their ability to access information in their first language and display it visually is already quite developed. As students apply strategies such s predicting, connecting, and guessing, they experience success and develop confidence in working with Kwak'wala materials. As students begin to make generalizations about what they see and hear, and to connect their experiences of Kwakwaka'wakw creative works to those of other cultures. As students talk and write about their own cultural experiences and engage in classroom cultural activities, they reveal the extent of their openness and interest through their participation and questions they ask.

1610205.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 7 students work with some memorized language and begin to connect ideas to form short interactions. They use writing for reference purposes and to develop their language skills, although accuracy of written expression is not assessed in grade 7. At this level, students begin to build on expressions and language they have acquired to produce connected messages. While many of the information tasks at this level continue to rely on visual representations, students are expected to use some basic well-practiced Kwak'wala vocabulary and language structures. Growing communicative skills permit them to begin to transfer and substitute language in the resources to suit their own purposes. Students in Grade 7 can recognize familiar language and can sometimes use their growing range of strategies to make educated guesses of the meaning of new expressions. Students benefit by having the opportunity to choose they way they respond to painting, song, dance,or video. Grade 7 students continue to explore and experience aspects of the Kwakwaka'wakew world as well as aspects of their family, school, and community traditions in order to see similarities that exist beneath surface differences.

1610206.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 8 students use Kwak'wala as a tool for communicating about every day topics they enjoy, such as themselves, their friends, and favourite activities. Students need a safe environment for language risk-taking and opportunities to practice and develop their language in new contexts and different groupings. At this level, students begin to use writing to express messages about everyday topics of interest, and use time expressions and conjunctions to link test or speech together. Grade 8 students are interested in finding information about things when there is a meaningful reason to do so. The purpose or task dictates what information is needed and students demonstrate their skills and strategies by how they use and present the information to complete the task. At this age, students benefit from opportunities to experience works by a variety of Kwakwaka'wakw artists and storytellers. They use their growing language skills to select and discuss their favourite characters or works of art. Grade 8 students explore the similarities and differences between traditional and contemporary activities and interests in the Kwakwaka'wakw world to increase their understanding of Kwakwaka'wakw culture.

1610207.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 9 students continue to focus on the communication of meaning, with increasing focus on student interaction. The focus of learning is is to convey and understand meaning of practical purposes in situations that are relevant to Grade 9 students, such as preparing a meal or buying a gift. At this level, grade 9 students continue to expand their range of techniques for producing connected messages to convey meaning. When involved in spontaneous speaking activities, the focus is on participation, enthusiasm, and willingness to take risk with language. Students use their language skills and learning strategies to acquire age-relevant information for practical purposes. Students are able to experience and respond to an increasing range of creative works. They reveal their development in the choices they make and by efforts to use Kwak'wala in informal situations, as well as in their oral, visual, and written work. With students' deepening understanding of Kwakwaka'wakw culture, they are motivated to continue their language learning and add new perspectives to thier views of the world.

1610208.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. Students in Grade 10 are increasingly able to engage in spontaneous interactions using vocabulary and structures they have internalized. They display a growing ability to take risks with language. Communicating meaning is still the central focus, with emphasis on practical and everyday use of meaning. Students' descriptive abilities at this level include linking and sequencing of narratives to better communicate meaning, using learned structures and vocabulary in new ways in spontaneous presentations. At this level students are able to use many strategies to identify key information in a variety of source materials. The tasks they perform frequently integrate all aspects of language learning. As students develop increasing facility with oral language, their methods of responding to creative works include artwork, visual displays, and oral or electronic presentations. Grade 10 learning activities support a growing awareness of Kwakwaka'wakww cultural practices and Kwak'wala idiomatic expressions.

1610209.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners to communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Introductory Kwak'wala 11, students begin to develop effective Kwak'wala language skills by focusing on an exchanging an increasing range of information, beginning with their interests and needs. As students progress in the Kwak'wala skills they apply a growing range of language learning strategies. Growing language skills enable students to gain facility in using a range of structures and sequencing techniques to produce connected speech and text. Introductory Kwak'wala 11 students are encouraged to use as many language-learning strategies as they can to extract key information from Kwak'wal resources to complete tasks. At this age, students can experience a much wider range of creative works, particularly stories and songs, that they are able to understand independently. Students are encouraged to respond creatively from a variety of options. Introductory Grade 11 students are encouraged to participate in a range of Kwakwaka'wakw cultural experiences. As their language abilities grow, students have frequent opportunities to interact in Kwak'wala and practice using appropriate communication conventions.

1610210.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. Students in Grade 11 are able to act with greater confidence in familiar situations and apply their growing range of communications strategies more consistently. At this level, students are able to speak and write with growing confidence and experiment with an increasing range of structures and sequencing techniques for practical purposes. By grade 11, students are familiar with a wide range of classroom, library, and other resources they can use to locate information. Many students are able to retrieve specific information and apply their growing language abilities to adapting information for a purpose. At this age, students are increasingly receptive to experiencing and interpreting creative works from a broader range of formats, time periods, and Kwakwaka'wakw regions. Through oral interactions and short written texts, students demonstrate and increasing level of sophistication in their responses.

1610211.publishedDescription: The study of Kwak'wala language and Kwakwaka'wakw culture is intended to enable learners ot communicate in Kwak'wala, and to provide opportunities for students to gain insights into their own cultures and to develop openness to cultural diversity. The Kwak'wala 5 to 12 curriculum is based upon the communicative-experiential approach in which the focus of instruction is the purposeful use of language to perform real-life tasks, to share ideas, to acquire information, and to get things done. Grammar plays a supportive role only - to provide useful strategies to facilitate communication and comprehension. In Grade 12, students are expected to engage in increasingly complex and spontaneous oral interactions in which they demonstrate their facility with Kwak'wala and the strategies they have developed to sustain and extend communication. Students are expected to produce increasingly complex connected speech and text by bringing together the writing and speaking skills they have learned and apply them to real-life situations. Grade 12 students are be able to locate information with confidence, skim for relevant facts, and use dictionaries and other resources appropriately. As students develop increasing facility with oral and written language, they are able to experience and interpret traditional and contemporary creative works in varied ways, with increasing detail and independence. In grade 12, students reveal their cultural understanding through daily activities and interactions by examining traditional and contemporary Kwakwaka'wakw culture in light of all their cultural experiences.

1901008.publishedDescription: Family and Society 10 is an ADST course that focuses on service design for individuals and families across their lifespan, including topics such as societal influences and impacts on families, the rights of children, and interpersonal relationships.

1901009.publishedDescription: Famille et société 10 is an ADST course that focuses on service design for individuals and families across their lifespan, including topics such as societal influences and impacts on families, the rights of children, and interpersonal relationships.

1901010.publishedDescription: Textiles 10 is an ADST course that focuses on basic techniques for textile creation and altering of patterns, including design principles, First Peoples traditional and current textile knowledge and practices, and environmental and ethical considerations of textile choices.

1901014.publishedDescription: This course focuses on simple textile design and creation, techniques in pattern modification, and repurposing of textile items. Learning includes the principles of design and textile functionality, use of symbolism in textile prints, and the economical and ethical factors involved in textile production, marketing, and consumption.

1901015.publishedDescription: This course focuses on design and creation of complex textile items, methods for developing patterns, and textile manipulation techniques. Learning includes First Peoples historical and current textile knowledge and practices, the design for the life cycle of textile products and its environmental impacts, and career opportunities in textile design, production, and distribution.

1901027.publishedDescription: This course focuses on meal design and preparation, recipe modification, and food systems and security. 2301142.publishedDescription: Literacy Foundations (LF) English Level 1/2: Companion - Speaking & Listening is the first of five companion courses that are designed to be supplemental to the LF English core courses. This course is intended for students who require substantial skill enhancement in the speaking and listening aspects of the English language and who need additional time to learn or solidify their speaking and listening skills before advancing in the LF English core courses. Prescribed Learning Outcomes are presented under four curriculum organizers: Purposes, Strategies, Thinking, and Features. The Prescribed Learning Outcomes address the skills needed at a very beginning level of oral language acquisition and include sustaining conversation for short periods of time and speaking clearly to enhance communication and meaning.

2301143.publishedDescription: Literacy Foundations (LF) English Level 1/2: Companion - Reading is the second of five companion courses that are designed to be supplemental to the LF English core courses. This course is intended for students who require substantial skill enhancement in the reading aspect of the English language and who need additional time to learn or solidify their reading skills before advancing in the LF English core courses. Prescribed Learning Outcomes are presented under four curriculum organizers: Purposes, Strategies, Thinking, and Features. The Prescribed Learning Outcomes address the skills needed at a very beginning level of reading and include recognizing word patterns and high-frequency words, and constructing meaning from brief, informational texts.

2301146.publishedDescription: Literacy Foundations (LF) English Level 3/4: Companion - Reading is the third of five companion courses that are designed to be supplemental to the LF English core courses. This course is intended for students who require substantial skill enhancement in the reading aspect of the English language and who need additional time to learn or solidify their reading skills before advancing in the LF English core courses. Prescribed Learning Outcomes are presented under four curriculum organizers: Purposes, Strategies, Thinking, and Features. The Prescribed Learning Outcomes address the skills needed at a developing to functional level of reading and include reading with fluency and demonstrating an understanding of the content in a variety of simple informational texts.

2301147.publishedDescription: Literacy Foundations (LF) English Level 3/4: Companion - Writing is the fourth of five companion courses that are designed to be supplemental to the LF English core courses. This course is intended for students who require substantial skill enhancement in the writing aspect of the English language and who need additional time to learn or solidify their writing skills before advancing in the LF English core courses. Prescribed Learning Outcomes are presented under four curriculum organizers: Purposes, Strategies, Thinking, and Features. The Prescribed Learning Outcomes address the skills needed at a developing to functional level of writing and include creating a variety of clear, personal, informational, and imaginative writings.

2301148.publishedDescription: Literacy Foundations (LF) English Level 5/6/7: Companion - Writing is the fifth of five companion courses that are designed to be supplemental to the LF English core courses. This course is intended for students who require substantial skill enhancement in the writing aspect of the English language and who need additional time to learn or solidify their writing skills before advancing in the LF English core courses. Prescribed Learning Outcomes are presented under four curriculum organizers: Purposes, Strategies, Thinking, and Features. The Prescribed Learning Outcomes address the skills needed at an increasingly sophisticated level of writing and include creating a variety of clear, personal, informational, and imaginative writings by using elements of style, structure, and form appropriate to purpose and audience.

2301154.publishedDescription: Independent directed study. The extended essay is intended to promote high-level research and writing skills, intellectual discovery and creativity. It provides students with an opportunity to engage in personal research in a topic of their own choice, under the guidance of a supervisor (a teacher in the school). This leads to a major piece of formally presented, structured writing, in which ideas and findings are communicated in a reasoned and coherent manner, appropriate to the subject chosen. It is recommended that completion of the written essay is followed by a short, concluding interview, or viva voce, with the supervisor. The extended essay, a substantial piece of writing of up to 4,000 words, enables students to investigate a topic of special interest that they have chosen themselves. It also encourages them to develop the skills of independent research that will be expected at university. Note: Student will receive English or French Instruction depending on who is the teacher supervisor.

2301159.publishedDescription: English Studies 12 is a comprehensive Language Arts course designed for all students that includes essential disciplinary learning at the Graduation level. It represents the culmination of all that has been learned throughout English Language Arts K-11. The course builds on and extends students? previous learning experiences in ELA and EFP 10 and 11 courses and provides students with opportunities to: - refine their ability to communicate effectively in a variety of contexts and to achieve their personal and career goals, - think critically and creatively about the uses of language, - explore texts from a variety of sources, in multiple modes, and that reflect diverse worldviews, - deepen their understanding of themselves and others in a changing world, - gain insight into the diverse factors that shape identity, - appreciate the importance of self-representation through text, - contribute to Reconciliation by building greater understanding of the knowledge and perspectives of First Peoples, - expand their understanding of what it means to be educated Canadian and global citizens.

2301160.programGuideTitle: English First Peoples - LITERARY STUDIES AND SPOKEN LANGUAGE 11 (2018)
2301160.publishedDescription: Literary Studies + Spoken Language 11 is designed for students who are interested in studying First Peoples? literature and oral tradition and in strengthening their oral language proficiencies. The course emphasizes oral self-expression and communication in a variety of contexts. Students delve deeply into First Peoples? oral and written literature in a range of media to explore various themes, authors, and topics. This provides a foundation for them to think critically and creatively as they continue to explore, extend, and strengthen their own repertoire of oral language expressions. Within a supportive community, students work individually and collaboratively to explore oral and written literature and create powerful, purposeful oral and written compositions. Through the study of literature and the processes of experimenting with, reflecting on, extending, and refining their oral language, students: - extend their capacity to communicate effectively in a variety of contexts, - deepen their understanding of themselves and the world, - expand their understanding of what it means to be educated Canadian and global citizens.

2301161.publishedDescription: English First Peoples 12 builds upon and extends students? previous learning experiences in ELA and EFP 10 and 11 courses. The course is grounded in the First Peoples? Principles of Learning. It is designed for all students, Aboriginal and non-Aboriginal, who are interested in delving deeply into First Peoples? oral and written literature and visual texts in a range of media. The course focusses on the experiences, values, beliefs, and lived realities of First Peoples as evidenced in various forms of text, including oral story, poetry, song, performance, film, and prose. A key feature of the course is its focus on authentic First Peoples voices (i.e., historical or contemporary texts created by or with First Peoples). In EFP 12, all students - examine texts grounded in a diversity of First Peoples? cultures, including local First Nations or Me´tis communities, - extend their capacity to communicate effectively in a variety of contexts, - think critically and creatively about the uses of language, - deepen their understanding of themselves and others in a changing world, - gain insight into the diverse factors that have shaped and continue to shape their own identities, - appreciate the importance of self-representation through authentic First Peoples? text, - contribute to Reconciliation by building greater understanding of the knowledge and perspectives of First Peoples, - expand their understandings of what it means to be educated Canadian and global citizens.

2301162.programGuideTitle: English First Peoples - LITERARY STUDIES AND NEW MEDIA 11 (2018)
2301162.publishedDescription: Literary Studies + New Media 11 is designed for students who are interested in studying First Peoples? literature and examining the evolving role of technology in today?s society, especially the increasing importance of digital media in communicating and exchanging ideas and engaging in social advocacy. Students delve deeply into First Peoples? oral and written literature in a range of media to explore various themes, authors, and topics. This provides a foundation for students to think critically and creatively as they continue to explore, extend, and strengthen their own writing and communication. Students examine the increasingly complex digital world and have opportunities to demonstrate understanding and communicate sophisticated ideas through a wide variety of digital and print media. Through the study of literature and critical engagement with new media, students: - extend their capacity to communicate effectively in a variety of contexts, - deepen their understanding of themselves and the world, - expand their understanding of what it means to be educated Canadian and global citizens.

2301163.publishedDescription: In Études littéraires et artistiques + nouveaux médias 10 students will discover a variety of texts and works from different eras, cultures, styles, and genres, and develop their ability to analyze, interpret, and evaluate in order to appreciate the formal and aesthetic qualities of a work. By studying a range of authors and artists, students will acquire a general culture that will contribute to the development of their Francophone identity. This course recognizes that digital literacy is an essential characteristic of the engaged and informed citizen. Students will reflect on how media changes our social behaviour, our understanding of the world, and our ways of communicating. Students will develop digital skills and a greater understanding of how human, cultural, and social issues relate to technology. They will also explore new possibilities for creative expression provided by digital media. Students will also be able to identify ethical, law-abiding behaviours that can help them make informed decisions throughout their lives.

2301164.publishedDescription: In Études littéraires et artistiques + expression orale 10 students will discover a variety of texts and works from different eras, cultures, styles, and genres, and develop their ability to analyze, interpret, and evaluate in order to appreciate the formal and aesthetic qualities of a work. By studying a range of authors and artists, students will acquire a general culture that will contribute to the development of their Francophone identity. Spoken communication, including that used in performance, will be explored in different contexts. Students will explore and compose a variety of oral texts, ranging from formal documents to creative writing and presentations. Students will be introduced to various types of oral expression and consider the benefits of non-verbal communication and active listening. Scenario-based simulations will reinforce to students the importance of the audience and the collaborative nature of communication. The course will enable them to explore, interpret, create, and present works for various purposes.

2301165.publishedDescription: Le cours d'Études littéraires et artistiques + nouveaux médias 11 (4 crédits) permettra à l'élève de découvrir une variété de textes et d??uvres issus d?époques, de cultures, de styles et de genres différents. De plus, il va de soi que la littératie numérique est une caractéristique essentielle du citoyen engagé et instruit. Dans ce cours, l'élève développera ses capacités d?analyse, d?interprétation et d?évaluation afin de mieux apprécier les qualités formelles et esthétiques d'une ?uvre. Par l?étude d?une variété d'auteurs et d?artistes, l'élève acquerra une culture générale qui contribuera au développement de son identité francophone. De plus, ces études ouvriront ses horizons de citoyen du monde. Alternativement ou simultanément à ce qui précède, l?élève sera amené à réfléchir sur la manière dont les médias changent nos comportements sociétaux, notre compréhension du monde et la manière dont nous communiquons. L?élève développera ses compétences numériques ainsi que sa compréhension des questions d?ordre humain, culturel et social liées à la technologie. De plus, il explorera les nouvelles possibilités d?expression créative qu?offrent les médias numériques. L?élève aura conscience des comportements conformes à la loi et à l?éthique afin de prendre des décisions éclairées qui lui serviront tout au long de sa vie.

2301166.publishedDescription: Le cours d?Études littéraires et artistiques + expression orale 11 (4 crédits) permettra à l?élève de découvrir une variété de textes et d??uvres issus d?époques, de cultures, de styles et de genres différents. De plus, ce cours vise à développer les techniques de la communication orale afin d?améliorer la confiance en soi de l?élève. Dans ce cours, l?élève développera ses capacités d?analyse, d?interprétation et d?évaluation afin de mieux apprécier les qualités formelles et esthétiques d?une ?uvre. Par l?étude d?une variété d?auteurs et d?artistes, l?élève acquerra une culture générale qui contribuera au développement de son identité francophone. De plus, ces études ouvriront ses horizons de citoyen du monde. Alternativement ou simultanément à ce qui précède, la prise de parole sera explorée dans différents contextes. L?élève sera initié à l?apport du non-verbal et de l?écoute active. Les mises en situation encourageront l?élève à prendre conscience de l?importance du destinataire et de la nature collaborative de la communication. Le cours offrira à l?élève la possibilité d?explorer et d?interpréter ainsi que de créer et de présenter des ?uvres à des fins variées.

2301167.publishedDescription: Independent directed study to produce an in-depth project which is the product of the students? own initiative and should reflect their personal experience. The reflective project is intended to promote high-level research, writing and extended communication skills, intellectual discovery and creativity through a variety of different approaches. The RP focuses on an ethical dilemma of an issue directly linked to the student?s career related study. In addition to a written essay, students keep a record of their reflections on the process of undertaking and completing the reflective project using the Reflections on planning and progress form (RPPF)

2301168.publishedDescription: Independent directed study to produce an in-depth project which is the product of the students? own initiative and should reflect their personal experience. The reflective project is intended to promote high-level research, writing and extended communication skills, intellectual discovery and creativity through a variety of different approaches. The RP focuses on an ethical dilemma of an issue directly linked to the student?s career related study. In addition to a written essay, students keep a record of their reflections on the process of undertaking and completing the reflective project using the Reflections on planning and progress form (RPPF).

2301169.publishedDescription: English Language Competency Assessment - listening, speaking, reading and writing.

2301170.publishedDescription: English Language Competency Assessment - listening, speaking, reading and writing.

2301171.publishedDescription: Composition 10 is designed to support students in their development of written communication through a critical process of questioning, exploring, and sampling. Within a supportive community of writers, students will work individually and collaboratively to explore and create coherent, purposeful compositions. Students will read and study compositions by other writers and consider a variety of styles as models for the development of their writing. The course builds students? writing competencies by introducing them to varied structures, forms, and styles of compositions. Students have opportunities to individually and collaboratively study, create, and write original pieces, exploring audience and purpose. They also develop their craft through processes of drafting, reflecting, and revising.

2301172.publishedDescription: Composition 11 is designed to support students as they refine, clarify, and adjust their written communication through practice and revision. Students will read and study compositions by other writers and be exposed to a variety of styles as models for the development of their writing. The course provides opportunities for students to, with increasing independence, study, create, and write original and authentic pieces for a range of purposes and real-world audiences. They will expand their competencies through processes of drafting, reflecting, and revising to build a body of work that demonstrates expanding breadth, depth, and evidence of writing for a range of situations. They will develop confidence in their abilities as they consolidate their writing craft.

2301173.publishedDescription: Composition 12 is designed to support students in their refinement and pursuit of mastery of written communication. Students will read and study exemplary compositions by other writers and be exposed to a variety of styles as models for the development of their writing. The course provides opportunities for students, with increasing independence and self-sufficiency, to study, create, and write original and authentic pieces for a range of purposes and audiences using real-world applications with impact and effectiveness. They will develop their craft through processes of drafting, reflecting, and revising to build a body of publishable work that demonstrates breadth, depth, and evidence of sophisticated and specialized writing for a range of situations.

2301174.publishedDescription: In Études littéraires et artistiques + production écrite 10 students will discover a variety of texts and works from different eras, cultures, styles, and genres, and develop their ability to analyze, interpret, and evaluate in order to appreciate the formal and aesthetic qualities of a work. By studying a range of authors and artists, students will acquire a general culture that will contribute to the development of their Francophone identity. This course recognizes that writing is key to becoming an engaged and informed citizen. Students will explore and compose a variety of texts, and will read and draw inspiration from a range of works and writing styles, which will serve as a model for their own writing. They will have the opportunity to study, create, and write original works, taking into account the audience and the purpose of the text. Through the process of writing, reflection, and editing, students will be introduced to the act of writing as a means of expression and thought.

2301191.publishedDescription: Creative Writing 10 is designed for students who have an interest in creative expression through language. The course provides students opportunities to build their writing skills through the exploration of identity, memory, and story in a range of genres. Within a supportive community of writers, students will collaborate and develop their skills through writing and design processes. This course is intentionally grounded in the sampling of writing processes, inviting students to express themselves creatively as they experiment with, reflect on, and practise their writing.

2301192.publishedDescription: Creative Writing 11 is designed for students who are interested in developing confidence and refining their writing skills through self- expression for various creative purposes. The course provides students with in-depth opportunities to explore personal and cultural identities, memories, and stories in a wide range of genres. Within a supportive community, students will collaborate and strengthen their skills through writing and design processes. Creative Writing 11 is grounded in the exploration and application of writing processes, inviting students to express themselves creatively as they reflect on, adjust, and extend their writing skills.

2301193.publishedDescription: EFP Writing 10 is designed for students who are interested in writing for a variety of purposes and contexts. This area of choice provides students with opportunities to become better writers through the exploration of personal and cultural identities, memories, stories, and connections to land/place. Within a supportive community, students will work individually and collaboratively to develop their writing skills and create coherent, purposeful, and engaging compositions. This course is grounded in the exploration and application of writing processes, inviting students to express themselves as they experiment with, reflect on, extend, and refine their writing.

2301194.publishedDescription: Creative Writing 12 is designed for students who are interested in creating a body of work reflective of a sophisticated breadth and depth of skill. The course provides students with opportunities to specialize and publish in real-world contexts. Students engage in the exploration of personal and cultural identities, memories, and stories, in a wide range of genres. Within a supportive community, students will collaborate and develop their skills through writing and design processes, celebrating successes. Students will refine their ability to write in complex, controlled styles with effectiveness and impact.

2301199.publishedDescription: Studies in English language and literature.

2301200.publishedDescription: Studies in English language and literature.

2301201.publishedDescription: Studies in English language and literature.

2301202.publishedDescription: Studies in English language and literature.

2301203.publishedDescription: Studies in English literature and performance.

2301204.publishedDescription: Studies in English literature and performance.

2301215.publishedDescription: Literary Studies 10 is designed for students who are interested in the literature of a particular era, geographical area, or theme, or in the study of literature in general. The course allows students to delve more deeply into literature as they explore specific themes, periods, authors, or areas of the world through literary works in a variety of media. Giving students the choice of a range of literary topics allows them to follow their passion and at the same time: - increase their literacy skills through close reading of appropriately challenging texts - enhance their development of the English Language Arts curricular competencies, both expressive and receptive - expand their development as educated global citizens - develop balance and broaden their understanding of themselves and the world - develop higher-level thinking and learning skills

2301217.publishedDescription: Literary Studies 11 allows students to delve deeply into literature. Students can explore specific themes, periods, authors, or areas of the world through literary works (fiction and non-fiction) in a variety of media. Giving students the choice of a range of literary topics allows them to follow their passion and at the same time: - increase their literacy skills through close reading of appropriately challenging texts, - enhance their development of the English Language Arts curricular competencies, both expressive and receptive, - expand their development as educated global citizens, - develop balance and broaden their understanding of themselves and the world, - develop higher-level thinking and learning skills.

2301218.publishedDescription: EFP Literary Studies 10 is designed for students who are interested in exploring First Peoples literature in a variety of contexts, genres, and media. This area of choice provides students with opportunities to explore personal and cultural identities, histories, stories, and connections to land/place. This course is grounded in the understanding of how texts are historically and culturally constructed. Students will work individually and collaboratively to broaden their understanding of themselves and the world.

2301221.programGuideTitle: English First Peoples - LITERARY STUDIES AND WRITING 11 (2018)
2301221.publishedDescription: EFP Literary Studies and Writing 11 is designed for students who are interested in studying First Peoples? literature and using writing for self-expression and communication in a variety of contexts. Students delve deeply into First Peoples? oral and written literature in a range of media to explore various themes, authors, and topics. This provides a foundation for them to think critically and creatively as they continue to explore, extend, and strengthen their own writing. Within a supportive community, students work individually and collaboratively to explore oral and written literature and create powerful, purposeful compositions. Through the study of literature and the processes of experimenting with, reflecting on, extending, and refining their writing, students: - extend their capacity to communicate effectively in a variety of contexts, - deepen their understanding of themselves and the world, - expand their understanding of what it means to be educated Canadian and global citizens.

2301224.publishedDescription: Literary Studies 12 allows students to delve more deeply into literature through increasingly complex texts. Students can explore specific themes, periods, authors, or areas of the world through literary works (fiction and non-fiction) in a variety of media. Giving students the choice of a range of literary topics allows them to follow their passion and at the same time: - increase their literacy skills through close reading of appropriately challenging texts, - enhance their development of the English Language Arts curricular competencies, both expressive and receptive, - expand their development as educated global citizens, - develop balance and broaden their understanding of themselves and the world, - further refine higher-level thinking and learning skills.

2301225.publishedDescription: Le cours d?Études littéraires et artistiques + production écrite 11 (4 crédits) permettra à l?élève de découvrir une variété de textes et d??uvres issus d?époques, de cultures, de styles et de genres différents. De plus, il va de soi que la production écrite est une caractéristique essentielle du citoyen engagé et instruit. Dans ce cours, l?élève développera ses capacités d?analyse, d?interprétation et d?évaluation afin de mieux apprécier les qualités formelles et esthétiques d?une ?uvre. Par l?étude d?une variété d?auteurs et d?artistes, l?élève acquerra une culture générale qui contribuera au développement de son identité francophone. De plus, ces études ouvriront ses horizons de citoyen du monde. Alternativement ou simultanément à ce qui précède, l?élève s?engagera dans l?exploration et la composition de diverses structures de textes écrits, allant de la rédaction formelle à la création et à la prestation. L?élève lira et s?inspirera de multiples ouvrages et il considérera une variété de styles comme modèles pour l?élaboration de ses productions. Il aura la possibilité d?étudier, de créer et d?écrire des ?uvres originales, en tenant compte du destinataire et de l?intention du texte. Par les processus de rédaction, de réflexion et de révision, l?élève s?initiera à l?acte d?écriture en tant que moyen de dire et de se dire.

2301227.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Grade 6 or 7 or 8

2301228.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Practical Grade 7 and Speech Arts Theory Level 1

2301229.publishedDescription: Report credit for: either Practical Grade 8 and Speech Arts History and Literature Theory Level 1 or Practical Grade 9 and Speech Arts Theory Level 2 plus Speech Arts History and Literature Theory Level 2

2304001.publishedDescription: Report credit for: Practical Grade 10 and Speech Arts Theory Level 3 plus Speech Arts History and Literature Theory Level 3

2305060.publishedDescription: Creative Writing 11 is designed for students who are interested in developing confidence and refining their writing skills through self- expression for various creative purposes. The course provides students with in-depth opportunities to explore personal and cultural identities, memories, and stories in a wide range of genres. Within a supportive community, students will collaborate and strengthen their skills through writing and design processes. Creative Writing 11 is grounded in the exploration and application of writing processes, inviting students to express themselves creatively as they reflect on, adjust, and extend their writing skills.

2305062.publishedDescription: Le cours de Communication orale 12 est conçu pour amener l?élève à peaufiner et préciser sa communication grâce à la pratique et à la révision de textes provenant de la culture francophone avec un accent mis sur la communication à l?oral. Le cours offre à l?élève la possibilité d?étudier des textes authentiques ainsi que de créer, d'écrire et de partager des textes originaux à des fins variées dans un contexte actuel. L?élève approfondira également ses compétences d'écoute active. À travers des processus de rédaction, de réflexion et de révision, l?élève concevra un ensemble de textes pour une variété de situations langagières. L?élève démontrera ses capacités à communiquer tant à l?oral qu?à l?écrit.

2305063.programGuideTitle: ÉTUDES DU CINÉMA ET DE LA LITTÉRATURE FRANCOPHONES 12 (2018)
2305063.publishedDescription: Le cours d?études du cinéma et de la littérature francophone 12 est conçu pour amener l?élève à analyser et interpréter en profondeur des oeuvres cinématographiques et littéraires de la francophonie. En étudiant une variété de textes, l'élève approfondira ses connaissances de la culture francophone contribuant au développement de son identité. La composante cinématographique permettra à l?élève d?examiner une variété d?oeuvres cinématographiques issue d?époques, de cultures, de styles et de genres différents. De plus, l?élève approfondira ses capacités d?analyse textuelle, d?interprétation et de réflexion afin de mieux apprécier et examiner par la critique des oeuvres cinématographiques de la francophonie. Plusieurs genres cinématographiques lui permettront d?explorer son imagination et de développer sa créativité, son style d?écriture et sa prise de parole. La composante littéraire permettra à l'élève d?examiner une variété de textes et d?oeuvres littéraires issus d?époques, de cultures, de styles et de genres différents. De plus, l'élève approfondira ses capacités d?analyse textuelle, d?interprétation et de réflexion afin de mieux apprécier et examiner par la critique des oeuvres littéraires de la francophonie. Plusieurs genres littéraires lui permettront d?explorer son imagination et de développer sa créativité et son style d?écriture.

2308009.programGuideTitle: ÉTUDES DU CINÉMA ET DE LA LITTÉRATURE FRANCOPHONES 11 (2018)
2308009.publishedDescription: Le cours d?études du cinéma et de la littérature francophone 11 est conçu pour encourager l?élève à découvrir, explorer, analyser et interpréter des oeuvres cinématographiques et littéraires de la francophonie. En étudiant une variété de textes, l'élève acquerra une certaine connaissance de la culture francophone qui contribuera au développement de son identité. La composante cinématographique permettra à l?élève de découvrir une variété d?oeuvres cinématographiques issues d?époques, de cultures, de styles et de genres différents. De plus, l?élève développera ses capacités d?analyse textuelle, d?interprétation et de réflexion afin de mieux apprécier les oeuvres cinématographiques de la francophonie. Plusieurs genres cinématographiques lui permettront d?explorer son imagination et de développer sa créativité, son style d?écriture et sa prise de parole. La composante littéraire permettra à l'élève de découvrir une variété de textes et d?oeuvres littéraires issus d?époques, de cultures, de styles et de genres différents. De plus, l'élève développera ses capacités d?analyse textuelle, d?interprétation et de réflexion afin de mieux apprécier les oeuvres littéraires de la francophonie. Plusieurs genres littéraires lui permettront d?explorer son imagination et de développer sa créativité et son style d?écriture.

2401077.publishedDescription: The course incorporates the traditional disciplines of anatomy and physiology, biomechanics, psychology and nutrition, which are studied in the context of sport, exercise and health. Students will cover a range of core and option topics and carry out practical (experimental) investigations in both laboratory and field settings. This will provide an opportunity to acquire the knowledge and understanding necessary to apply scientific principles and critically analyse human performance. Where relevant, the course will address issues of internationalism and ethics by considering sport, exercise and health relative to the individual and in a global context.
